label: Google Calendar
description: Read, create, and manage events on your Google Calendars.

form.title: Connect Google Calendar
form.subtitle: Authorize Anton to read and manage events on your calendars.

methods.label: In-Browser Connect
methods.description: Opens Google sign-in in your browser. No credentials required — just click Connect.

methods.label: Sign in with Google
methods.description: OAuth 2.0 — a browser pops open for you to grant Anton scoped access to your calendars. No passwords stored, revocable from your Google Account at any time. Currently requires you to bring your own Google Cloud OAuth client (steps below); we'll switch to a hosted client in a future release.
methods.how_to:
## Sign in with Google (OAuth 2.0)

The modern, recommended path. Anton spawns a tiny local server, opens Google's consent screen in your browser, and exchanges the resulting authorization code for a refresh token — never sent to Anton's servers.

### Pattern A — using Anton's hosted OAuth client

When the connector spec ships with a `client_id`, you don't see any fields here. Click **Submit** and:

1. Your default browser opens to Google's consent screen.
2. Pick the Google account whose calendars you want Anton to use.
3. Review the requested permissions:
   - **See, edit, share, and permanently delete all the calendars you can access** (`calendar`)
   - **See your email address** (`userinfo.email`)
4. Click **Continue**. The browser hands a one-time authorization code back to Anton through a `127.0.0.1` redirect.
5. Anton exchanges the code (using PKCE — no client secret needed) for a refresh token and stores it locally.

### Pattern B — bring your own OAuth client

If this method shows two fields below (**Client ID** and **Client secret**), Anton doesn't ship a hosted OAuth client for Google Calendar yet — you supply your own from Google Cloud Console:

1. Open [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/) → pick or create a project.
2. **APIs & Services → Library** → enable **Google Calendar API**.
3. **APIs & Services → OAuth consent screen** → set up an internal or external app, add your email as a test user.
4. **APIs & Services → Credentials → Create credentials → OAuth client ID** → pick **Desktop app** as the type.
5. Copy the generated **Client ID** and **Client secret** into the fields below.
6. Click **Submit**. Anton runs the same browser flow with your client.

### Limiting the scope

The spec requests `calendar` (full read/write across all your calendars). If you'd rather only let Anton manage events (not create new calendars), edit the connector spec and switch to `calendar.events`. For read-only, use `calendar.readonly`.

### Revoking later

Visit [Third-party apps with account access](https://myaccount.google.com/permissions), find the entry, and click **Remove access**. The next task that hits Calendar will fail with an auth error and prompt you to re-authorize.

fields.name: client_id
fields.label: OAuth client ID
fields.description: From Google Cloud Console → APIs & Services → Credentials. Pick the Desktop-app OAuth client.

fields.name: client_secret
fields.label: OAuth client secret
fields.description: Paired with the client ID; copied from the same row in Cloud Console.

methods.label: Service account
methods.description: Google Workspace admins only. Uses a service-account JSON key with domain-wide delegation to act on behalf of any user in your domain.
methods.how_to:
## Service account with domain-wide delegation

For Google Workspace admins who want Anton to operate on calendars across the org without each user authorizing individually. Skip this method for personal Google accounts — use **Sign in with Google** instead.

### Steps

1. In [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/), create or pick a project.
2. Enable the **Google Calendar API** under **APIs & Services → Library**.
3. Go to **IAM & Admin → Service Accounts → Create service account**. Give it a name like `anton-calendar`.
4. Open the service account, switch to the **Keys** tab, and **Add key → Create new key → JSON**. Download the file.
5. Note the service account's **Unique ID** (a 21-digit number on the Details tab).
6. In [Google Workspace Admin Console](https://admin.google.com), open **Security → Access and data control → API controls → Domain-wide delegation**.
7. Click **Add new** and use the Unique ID from step 5. Add these scopes:
   - `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar`
   - `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email`
8. Paste the JSON key file's full contents into **Service account JSON** below.
9. Set **Impersonate email** to the user account Anton should act as.

### Notes

- The JSON key is the security boundary — anyone with it can impersonate every user in the delegation list. Rotate keys in Cloud Console if you suspect exposure.
- Rotating invalidates the key Anton has stored. Re-paste the new JSON to refresh.
- Read [Google's domain-wide delegation guide](https://developers.google.com/identity/protocols/oauth2/service-account#delegatingauthority).

fields.name: service_account_json
fields.label: Service account JSON
fields.description: Paste the entire contents of the JSON key file.

fields.name: impersonate_email
fields.label: Impersonate email
fields.description: The user account Anton acts as — must belong to your Workspace domain.
